scriptFull:
Once the shock subsided, the security community ran its post-mortem. And the conclusion was uncomfortable: the chaos had little to do with the sophistication of the attack. It had everything to do with an organizational failure to cope with a threat that should have been manageable.
The Stryker attack was not a brilliant zero-day exploit. There was no exotic malware, no cunning obfuscation. What it exploited was an architectural decision — that a single administrative account should hold permanent, always-on, global power over two hundred thousand devices.
One account. Two hundred thousand devices. No approval chain. No second signature. No expiration on the privilege.
The concept that stands against this is called Just-in-Time access. Under this model, administrators hold zero permissions by default. When they need to perform an elevated action — deploy a patch, run a diagnostic, wipe a device — they request that access through a formal workflow, for a specific task, for a specific window of time. The access is granted, the task is performed, the privilege expires. An attacker who steals your session token inherits only the permissions you held in that precise moment — not a standing, permanent, global kill-switch.
Microsoft's own identity platform offers exactly this capability. It is not a third-party add-on. It is a native feature of the same Microsoft ecosystem Stryker was already using. Whether it was implemented — and if not, why not — will be central to the post-incident analysis for years.
The second failure is almost embarrassingly simple: there was no multi-person authorization for a destructive command. Nuclear launch protocols require two officers to simultaneously turn their keys. Major financial transfers require dual authorization. The ability to simultaneously factory-reset every device in a fifty-six-thousand-person global company is — by any reasonable definition — catastrophic and irreversible. It should require more than one administrator to authorize. It should require a confirmation step that creates even a brief window for an alert system to fire.
It did not. Because convenience, accumulated across years of small decisions, had won out over friction. Because no one seriously imagined, until it happened, that this button would ever be pressed by the wrong hands.
The authentication failure is equally specific. Adversary-in-the-Middle phishing defeats standard multi-factor authentication — the industry has known this for years. The solution exists: hardware security keys are cryptographically bound to the legitimate domain. A relay cannot intercept them because there is nothing to intercept — the handshake between the key and the server cannot be faked or forwarded. They are called phishing-resistant for a reason. They are also the least deployed protection for the highest-privilege accounts, which is precisely where the stakes are greatest.
The tools to prevent this existed before it happened. Some were native features of platforms the organization already paid for. The gap was not technical. It was the accumulation of deferred decisions — and the universal human tendency to weigh convenience over the risk, to treat an unlikely disaster as someone else's problem. Until it isn't.
